pattern: Bumping every element from the bottom up to some depth is really just bumping a prefix of an array, so a plain capped array with push/pop from the end handles the whole class in constant time per push and pop.
hints: Store the stack as a plain array; push refuses to grow past the fixed capacity, and pop removes (and returns) the last element or -1 if there is nothing to remove.
increment(k, val) only ever touches the bottom-most elements, so it adds val to indices 0..min(k, currentSize)-1 — never more than the stack currently holds.
Do the increment eagerly (walk the affected prefix and add val to each) rather than trying to defer it; with the given call limits a direct prefix walk is fast enough and keeps push/pop untouched.

edge_inputs.description: push past capacity is silently dropped, then normal pop/increment behavior resumes
increment on an empty stack does nothing and later pushes are unaffected
capacity of one: single push/pop cycle
increment with k far larger than the current stack size bumps every element, not more
pop on an empty stack must return -1 without raising, and popping does not affect maxSize
two increments in a row over overlapping but different-sized prefixes must both take effect additively

stress.description: long randomized mix of push, pop, and increment against a small-capacity stack
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    cap = rng.randint(1, 10)
    ops = ['CustomStack']
    args = [[cap]]
    count = 0
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        r = rng.random()
        if r < 0.4:
            ops.append('push')
            args.append([rng.randint(1, 1000)])
            count += 1
        elif r < 0.7:
            ops.append('pop')
            args.append([])
            count = max(0, count - 1)
        else:
            ops.append('increment')
            args.append([rng.randint(1, cap + 3), rng.randint(0, 100)])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: small capacity forces frequent full-stack pushes and empty-stack pops alongside increments
